title: 7 Supplement Combinations You Should Never Take Together
metaDescription: Some supplements cancel each other out or cause side effects when combined. Here are 7 pairs you need to watch out for and what to do instead.
intro: Not all supplements play nice together. While most are safe to combine, certain pairs can block absorption, cause side effects, or flat-out cancel each other's benefits. Here are seven combinations to avoid -- and what to do if you're taking both.

sections.heading: 1. Calcium and Iron
sections.body:
Calcium is one of the strongest inhibitors of iron absorption. Studies show that even 300mg of calcium can reduce iron uptake by up to 50%. This is especially problematic for people supplementing iron for anemia.

**What to do:** Take iron in the morning on an empty stomach and calcium in the evening with dinner. Space them at least 2 hours apart.

sections.heading: 2. Magnesium and Zinc (at the same time)
sections.body:
Both minerals compete for the same absorption pathways in your gut. Taking high doses together means you're shortchanging both. This is one of the most common stacking mistakes.

**What to do:** Take zinc with breakfast and magnesium before bed. This also plays to their strengths -- zinc supports daytime immunity while magnesium promotes evening relaxation.

sections.heading: 3. Vitamin C and B12
sections.body:
High-dose vitamin C (over 1000mg) can degrade vitamin B12 in your digestive tract, reducing the amount you actually absorb. If you're supplementing B12 for energy or to correct a deficiency, this matters.

**What to do:** Take B12 in the morning and vitamin C later in the day, or at least space them by 2 hours.

sections.heading: 4. Green Tea Extract and Iron
sections.body:
The tannins and polyphenols in green tea extract (EGCG) bind to iron and dramatically reduce absorption. This applies to green tea beverages too. One study found that tea consumed with a meal reduced iron absorption by 64%.

**What to do:** Take iron supplements at least 2 hours before or after green tea extract. If you drink green tea, avoid it around iron-rich meals.

sections.heading: 5. Vitamin E and Vitamin K
sections.body:
High-dose vitamin E can interfere with vitamin K's role in blood clotting. This is especially risky for people on blood thinners. Even without medication, mega-dosing vitamin E while taking K2 for bone health can create an imbalance.

**What to do:** Keep vitamin E at reasonable doses (under 400 IU). If you take both, don't mega-dose either one.

sections.heading: 6. Copper and Zinc
sections.body:
Long-term zinc supplementation (especially above 30mg/day) depletes copper levels. Copper deficiency can cause anemia, neurological problems, and weakened immunity -- the opposite of what you're trying to achieve with zinc.

**What to do:** If you take more than 30mg of zinc daily, add 1-2mg of copper. Many quality zinc supplements already include copper for this reason.

sections.heading: 7. Fish Oil and Ginkgo Biloba
sections.body:
Both have blood-thinning properties. Combining them increases the risk of bleeding, especially if you're already on aspirin or anticoagulant medication. Individually they're fine, but stacking multiple blood thinners adds up.

**What to do:** If you take both, consult your doctor -- especially before any surgery. Consider whether you need both or if one covers your goals.

sections.heading: The Bottom Line
sections.body:
Most supplement interactions aren't dangerous -- they just waste your money by reducing absorption. The fix is usually simple: space them apart by 2 hours or take them at different meals.

Use our [Supplement Checker](/) to instantly flag interactions in your full stack.

title: The Best Supplement Stack for Sleep in 2026
metaDescription: Build the ultimate sleep stack with magnesium, glycine, L-theanine, and more. Evidence-based recommendations for falling asleep faster and sleeping deeper.
intro: If you've tried melatonin and it's not cutting it, you're not alone. A proper sleep stack targets multiple pathways -- muscle relaxation, GABA activity, cortisol reduction, and circadian rhythm support. Here's the evidence-based stack that actually works.

sections.heading: The Core Stack
sections.body:
These four supplements form the foundation of an effective sleep protocol:

- **Magnesium Glycinate (400mg)** -- Activates GABA receptors, relaxes muscles, and the glycinate form itself has calming properties. This is the single most impactful sleep supplement for most people.
- **L-Theanine (200mg)** -- An amino acid from green tea that increases alpha brain waves. Promotes calm focus during the day and smooth sleep onset at night. No grogginess.
- **Glycine (3g)** -- Lowers core body temperature (a key sleep trigger) and improves sleep quality. The dose matters -- 3g is what the studies used.
- **Apigenin (50mg)** -- A flavonoid found in chamomile that binds to GABA receptors. Andrew Huberman popularized this one, and the research supports its mild sedative effect.

sections.heading: Optional Add-Ons
sections.body:
Depending on your specific sleep issues, consider adding one of these:

- **Ashwagandha (300mg KSM-66)** -- If stress or racing thoughts keep you awake. Reduces cortisol by up to 30% in studies.
- **Tart Cherry Extract (500mg)** -- A natural source of melatonin plus anti-inflammatory compounds. Good alternative to synthetic melatonin.
- **Melatonin (0.3-0.5mg)** -- Note the dose. Most people take way too much. The physiological dose is 0.3mg, not the 5-10mg pills sold everywhere. More is not better with melatonin.

sections.heading: Timing Protocol
sections.body:
Timing matters as much as the supplements themselves:

- **60 minutes before bed:** Magnesium glycinate + glycine
- **30 minutes before bed:** L-theanine + apigenin
- **If using melatonin:** 30 minutes before your target sleep time
- **Ashwagandha:** With dinner (needs food for absorption)

Take them at the same time every night. Consistency trains your body to associate the routine with sleep onset.

sections.heading: What to Avoid Before Bed
sections.body:
Your stack won't work if you're fighting against these:

- **Caffeine after 2pm** -- Half-life is 5-6 hours. That 3pm coffee is still 50% active at 9pm.
- **B vitamins at night** -- They're energizing. Move your B-complex to morning.
- **High-dose vitamin D at night** -- Some evidence it suppresses melatonin production. Take it in the morning.
- **Screens without blue blockers** -- Blue light suppresses melatonin by up to 50%. Use Night Shift, f.lux, or blue-blocking glasses after sunset.

sections.heading: Expected Results
sections.body:
Most people notice improvements within 3-5 days. Magnesium and glycine tend to work the fastest. L-theanine and apigenin build up over 1-2 weeks.

This stack targets sleep from multiple angles -- muscle relaxation, neurotransmitter support, temperature regulation, and cortisol management. That's why it works better than any single supplement alone.

title: The Best Supplement Stack for the Gym in 2026
metaDescription: The evidence-based gym supplement stack: creatine, caffeine, citrulline, and more. What actually works for strength, endurance, and recovery.
intro: The supplement industry sells a lot of garbage for gym performance. But a handful of supplements have rock-solid evidence behind them. Here's what actually moves the needle -- and what's just marketing.

sections.heading: Tier 1: The Non-Negotiables
sections.body:
If you only take three supplements for the gym, make it these:

- **Creatine Monohydrate (5g/day)** -- The most studied supplement in history. Increases strength, power output, and muscle hydration. Works for everyone from beginners to elite athletes. Take it daily -- timing doesn't matter, consistency does. Skip the loading phase.
- **Caffeine (200-400mg)** -- Improves strength, endurance, power output, and reduces perceived effort. Take 30-60 minutes pre-workout. Don't exceed 400mg or take it after 2pm.
- **Citrulline Malate (6-8g)** -- Boosts nitric oxide production for better blood flow and pumps. Also reduces muscle soreness by up to 40% in studies. Take 30 minutes pre-workout.

sections.heading: Tier 2: Strong Evidence
sections.body:
These have good research but are less critical than Tier 1:

- **Beta-Alanine (3-5g/day)** -- Buffers lactic acid, improving performance in the 1-4 minute effort range (think high-rep sets and HIIT). Causes a harmless tingling sensation. Works through daily saturation, not acute dosing.
- **Protein (whey/casein, 1.6-2.2g/kg bodyweight total)** -- Not glamorous, but most people don't eat enough protein. Whey post-workout, casein before bed if you need the extra protein.
- **Omega-3 (2-3g EPA+DHA)** -- Reduces exercise-induced inflammation and muscle soreness. Not a performance booster, but speeds recovery between sessions.

sections.heading: Tier 3: Situational
sections.body:
Worth considering depending on your goals:

- **Ashwagandha (600mg KSM-66)** -- Shown to improve strength gains and recovery in resistance-trained men. May boost testosterone modestly. Best for high-stress lifters.
- **Electrolytes** -- If you sweat heavily or train fasted. Sodium, potassium, magnesium. Skip the sugary sports drinks and use a clean electrolyte mix.
- **Vitamin D (2000-5000 IU)** -- If you're deficient (most people are). Low vitamin D is linked to impaired muscle function and recovery.

sections.heading: What's Overhyped
sections.body:
Save your money on these:

- **BCAAs** -- Useless if you eat enough protein. You're paying for 3 amino acids that are already in your protein shake.
- **Testosterone boosters** -- The ones sold as supplements don't work. Tribulus, D-aspartic acid, fenugreek -- none produce meaningful testosterone increases in healthy adults.
- **Pre-workout blends** -- Most are just caffeine with pixie-dusted doses of other ingredients. Buy the ingredients separately at proper doses.
- **Glutamine** -- Only useful if you're severely ill or burned. Your body makes plenty for normal exercise recovery.

sections.heading: Sample Pre-Workout Protocol
sections.body:
**60 minutes before training:**
- 200-400mg caffeine
- 6-8g citrulline malate
- 3g beta-alanine

**Daily (any time):**
- 5g creatine
- 2-3g omega-3

**Post-workout:**
- 25-40g whey protein

This is a no-nonsense stack based on what the research actually shows. Run it through our [Supplement Checker](/) to make sure it fits with everything else you take.

title: Vitamin D: The Complete Supplement Guide (2026)
metaDescription: Everything you need to know about vitamin D supplementation: optimal dose, best form, when to take it, what to pair it with, and signs of deficiency.
intro: Vitamin D deficiency affects an estimated 42% of American adults. It's involved in over 200 biological processes and its impact goes far beyond bone health. Here's the definitive guide to supplementing it correctly.

sections.heading: Why Most People Are Deficient
sections.body:
Your skin produces vitamin D from UVB sunlight, but modern life works against this:

- **Office jobs** mean limited sun exposure during peak UV hours (10am-2pm)
- **Northern latitudes** (above 37th parallel) get insufficient UVB for 4-6 months per year
- **Sunscreen** blocks 95-99% of UVB rays
- **Darker skin** requires 3-5x more sun exposure to produce the same amount
- **Age** reduces your skin's ability to synthesize vitamin D

Blood levels below 30 ng/mL are considered deficient. Below 20 ng/mL is severely deficient. Optimal is 40-60 ng/mL.

sections.heading: How Much to Take
sections.body:
The RDA of 600 IU is widely considered too low by researchers. Here's what the evidence supports:

- **Maintenance (if not deficient):** 2,000 IU/day
- **Correcting deficiency:** 4,000-5,000 IU/day for 2-3 months, then retest
- **Upper safe limit:** 4,000 IU/day (some researchers argue 10,000 IU is safe)

The only way to know your ideal dose is to test your blood levels. A 25-hydroxyvitamin D test costs $30-50 and most doctors will order one if you ask.

sections.heading: D3 vs D2
sections.body:
Always choose **vitamin D3 (cholecalciferol)**, not D2 (ergocalciferol).

D3 is the form your skin naturally produces and is 87% more effective at raising blood levels than D2. D2 is plant-derived and has a shorter shelf life. Most prescription vitamin D is D2 -- ask your doctor to specify D3 instead.

sections.heading: Essential Pairing: Vitamin K2
sections.body:
This is critical. Vitamin D increases calcium absorption from your gut, but without K2, that calcium can end up in your arteries instead of your bones.

**K2 (specifically MK-7)** activates proteins that direct calcium to bones and teeth while keeping it out of soft tissues. Take 100-200mcg of MK-7 for every 2,000-5,000 IU of D3.

Check our [Vitamin D + K2 pair page](/stack/vitamin-d-k2) for the full breakdown.

sections.heading: When and How to Take It
sections.body:
- **With food** -- D3 is fat-soluble. Taking it with a meal containing fat increases absorption by 32-50%.
- **Morning** -- Some evidence suggests evening doses may interfere with melatonin production.
- **Consistency** -- Daily dosing is better than weekly mega-doses for maintaining stable blood levels.

Good forms: softgels (already in oil), liquid drops, or tablets taken with a fatty meal. Avoid gummies -- they often have lower actual content than labeled.

sections.heading: Other Synergies and Conflicts
sections.body:
**Pairs well with:**
- Magnesium (D3 requires magnesium for activation)
- Omega-3 (fat-soluble, absorbs better together)
- Boron (extends vitamin D's half-life)

**Watch out for:**
- Calcium (take D3 to improve calcium absorption, but don't mega-dose calcium)
- Steroids/corticosteroids (can impair vitamin D metabolism)

Run your full stack through the [Supplement Checker](/) to see how vitamin D interacts with everything you take.

title: The Best Supplement Stack for Focus and Productivity
metaDescription: Build a nootropic stack that actually works. Evidence-based supplements for focus, memory, and sustained mental energy without the crash.
intro: The nootropics space is full of hype and pseudo-science. But a few supplements have legitimate evidence for improving focus, memory, and mental clarity. Here's a stack built on research, not Reddit hype.

sections.heading: The Foundation: Caffeine + L-Theanine
sections.body:
This is the most well-validated nootropic combination in existence.

- **Caffeine (100-200mg)** provides alertness and focus
- **L-Theanine (200mg)** smooths out the jitters, promotes calm focus, and prevents the crash

The 1:2 ratio (caffeine to theanine) is key. Studies show this combination improves attention, task-switching speed, and reduces susceptibility to distraction better than either alone.

Start with 100mg caffeine + 200mg L-theanine if you're caffeine-sensitive.

sections.heading: Cholinergic Support
sections.body:
Acetylcholine is the neurotransmitter most associated with learning and memory. Most people don't get enough choline from diet.

- **Alpha-GPC (300-600mg)** -- The most bioavailable choline source. Crosses the blood-brain barrier efficiently. Shown to improve memory and attention in studies.
- **Alternative: CDP-Choline/Citicoline (250-500mg)** -- Slightly different mechanism, also effective. Some people respond better to one or the other.

Pick one, not both. Start with Alpha-GPC.

sections.heading: Neuroplasticity and Long-Term Brain Health
sections.body:
These support brain health over weeks and months:

- **Lion's Mane Mushroom (500-1000mg)** -- Stimulates nerve growth factor (NGF) production. The only legal supplement shown to promote neurogenesis. Effects build over 2-4 weeks.
- **Omega-3 DHA (1-2g)** -- Your brain is 60% fat, and DHA is the primary structural omega-3 in brain tissue. Essential for membrane fluidity and neurotransmitter function.
- **Creatine (5g)** -- Yes, the gym supplement. Your brain uses creatine for ATP production. Studies show 5g/day improves working memory and processing speed, especially under stress or sleep deprivation.

sections.heading: Situational Add-Ons
sections.body:
Use these for specific situations:

- **Rhodiola Rosea (200-400mg)** -- Adaptogen that combats mental fatigue. Best for high-stress periods like deadlines or exams. Take it in the morning.
- **Bacopa Monnieri (300mg)** -- Improves memory consolidation. Takes 8-12 weeks to reach full effect. Better for students or anyone who needs to retain information.
- **Tyrosine (500-1000mg)** -- Precursor to dopamine. Useful for maintaining focus during sleep deprivation or high-stress situations. Take on an empty stomach.

sections.heading: Daily Protocol
sections.body:
**Morning (with breakfast):**
- 100-200mg caffeine + 200mg L-theanine
- 300mg Alpha-GPC
- 500mg Lion's Mane
- 5g creatine

**With lunch:**
- 1-2g omega-3 (DHA-heavy)

**As needed:**
- Rhodiola for high-stress days
- Extra L-theanine for afternoon focus without more caffeine

This stack works through multiple mechanisms -- alertness, acetylcholine support, neuroplasticity, and stress resilience. That's why it outperforms any single nootropic.

Check your full stack for interactions with our [Supplement Checker](/).

title: Supplement Guide for Beginners: Where to Start in 2026
metaDescription: New to supplements? Here's exactly what to take, what to skip, and how to build your first stack without wasting money.
intro: The supplement industry is worth $60 billion and most of it is unnecessary. If you're just starting out, the signal-to-noise ratio is terrible. This guide cuts through the marketing and tells you exactly what's worth your money.

sections.heading: The Big 3: What Almost Everyone Should Take
sections.body:
Before anything else, these three cover the most common deficiencies in modern diets:

**1. Vitamin D3 (2,000-4,000 IU/day)**
42% of Americans are deficient. Supports immunity, mood, hormones, bone health, and muscle function. Take with a fatty meal in the morning. Always pair with K2.

**2. Omega-3 / Fish Oil (2-3g EPA+DHA)**
Most people get too many omega-6s and not enough omega-3s. Supports brain health, reduces inflammation, and improves cardiovascular markers. Look for a high EPA+DHA concentration -- not total fish oil.

**3. Magnesium (300-400mg)**
Involved in 300+ enzymatic reactions. Deficiency causes poor sleep, muscle cramps, anxiety, and fatigue. Magnesium glycinate is the best all-around form. Take in the evening.

sections.heading: The Next Tier: Based on Your Goals
sections.body:
Once you have the Big 3 covered, add based on what you're trying to achieve:

**For sleep:** L-theanine, glycine, apigenin
**For gym performance:** Creatine, caffeine, citrulline
**For focus:** Caffeine + L-theanine, Alpha-GPC, Lion's Mane
**For stress:** Ashwagandha, rhodiola
**For longevity:** NAC + glycine (GlyNAC), CoQ10

Don't add everything at once. Introduce one new supplement every 1-2 weeks so you can tell what's actually working.

sections.heading: How to Buy Quality Supplements
sections.body:
The supplement industry is poorly regulated. Here's how to avoid garbage:

- **Look for third-party testing** -- NSF, USP, or Informed Sport certifications mean the product actually contains what the label says.
- **Avoid proprietary blends** -- If the label says "proprietary blend" instead of listing individual doses, the company is hiding something (usually under-dosing).
- **Check the form** -- Magnesium oxide is cheap but poorly absorbed. Magnesium glycinate costs more but actually works. The form matters for most minerals and some vitamins.
- **Skip the gummies** -- Lower doses, added sugar, less stable. Capsules or softgels are almost always better.
- **Buy from reputable brands** -- Thorne, NOW Foods, Jarrow, Life Extension, and Momentous have good track records.

sections.heading: Common Beginner Mistakes
sections.body:
**Taking too much at once.** Start with the Big 3 for 2-4 weeks before adding anything else. You need a baseline to measure against.

**Ignoring timing.** Taking magnesium in the morning and caffeine at night is working against yourself. Morning: vitamin D, omega-3, caffeine. Evening: magnesium, glycine, ashwagandha.

**Expecting instant results.** Some supplements work fast (caffeine, magnesium). Others take weeks (omega-3, lion's mane, creatine). Give each one at least 4 weeks before judging.

**Not checking interactions.** Even common supplements can interfere with each other. Calcium blocks iron. Zinc depletes copper. Use our [Supplement Checker](/) before stacking.

**Trusting influencer recommendations.** Most supplement influencers are paid affiliates. Look for recommendations backed by cited human studies, not "this brand changed my life" testimonials.

sections.heading: Your Starter Stack
sections.body:
Here's exactly what to buy for your first month:

| Supplement | Dose | When | Cost/mo |
|-----------|------|------|---------|
| Vitamin D3 + K2 | 2000 IU + 100mcg | Morning with food | ~$12 |
| Omega-3 | 2g EPA+DHA | With lunch | ~$15 |
| Magnesium glycinate | 400mg | Evening | ~$10 |

**Total: ~$37/month** for the highest-impact supplements. Add from there based on your goals.

Run any stack through our [Supplement Checker](/) to verify there are no conflicts before you buy.

title: Why Does Magnesium Make Me Sleepy?
metaDescription: Magnesium makes you sleepy because it activates GABA receptors, relaxes muscles, and lowers cortisol. Here's the science behind it and how to use it for sleep.
intro: You took magnesium and now you can barely keep your eyes open. You're not imagining it -- magnesium genuinely makes many people drowsy. Here's exactly why, and how to use this to your advantage (or avoid it).

sections.heading: The 3 Reasons Magnesium Makes You Sleepy
sections.body:
**1. GABA receptor activation.** Magnesium binds to GABA-A receptors in your brain -- the same receptors targeted by sleep medications like Ambien and benzodiazepines. GABA is your brain's primary "calm down" neurotransmitter. Magnesium enhances its effect.

**2. Cortisol reduction.** Magnesium helps regulate the HPA axis (your stress response system). Studies show it lowers cortisol levels, especially in people who are deficient. Lower cortisol = easier relaxation.

**3. Muscle relaxation.** Magnesium blocks calcium from entering muscle cells, which prevents contraction. This is why it helps with cramps and restless legs -- but it also creates a full-body relaxation effect that signals your body it's time to sleep.

sections.heading: The Form Matters
sections.body:
Not all magnesium forms are equally sedating:

- **Magnesium glycinate** -- Most calming. The glycine amino acid itself is a calming neurotransmitter. Double sleep benefit.
- **Magnesium threonate** -- Crosses the blood-brain barrier. Also quite sedating for many people.
- **Magnesium citrate** -- Moderate. More of a gut/absorption focus.
- **Magnesium oxide** -- Least sedating but also poorly absorbed. Mostly used as a laxative.

If magnesium is making you too sleepy during the day, switch to citrate or oxide in the morning and save glycinate for bedtime.

sections.heading: How to Use This for Better Sleep
sections.body:
If you want to harness the sleepiness:

- Take **400mg magnesium glycinate** 30-60 minutes before bed
- Pair with **200mg L-theanine** for amplified calm
- Add **3g glycine** for a triple GABA boost
- Keep your room cool -- magnesium also helps lower core body temperature

This is the foundation of our [Sleep Stack](/blog/best-supplement-stack-for-sleep). Check our [Supplement Checker](/) to make sure magnesium plays well with everything else you take.

sections.heading: What If I Need Magnesium But Don't Want Drowsiness?
sections.body:
Take it in the evening instead of the morning. Problem solved for most people.

If you need daytime magnesium (for cramps, migraines, etc.), use **magnesium citrate** or **malate** -- these forms are less sedating. Magnesium malate is actually preferred by some people for daytime energy support.

Avoid magnesium glycinate and threonate during the day if drowsiness is an issue.

title: Which Supplements Can You Take on an Empty Stomach?
metaDescription: Some supplements work better on an empty stomach, others need food. Here's the complete guide to which supplements to take with and without food.
intro: Taking supplements with or without food isn't just a suggestion -- it can make a 50% difference in absorption. Some supplements need stomach acid and fat, while others compete with food for absorption. Here's the definitive list.

sections.heading: Take on an Empty Stomach
sections.body:
These absorb best without food interference:

- **Iron** -- Food reduces iron absorption by up to 50%. Take first thing in the morning, 30-60 minutes before breakfast. Pair with vitamin C to boost absorption.
- **NAC (N-acetyl cysteine)** -- Competes with amino acids in food for absorption. Take 30 minutes before meals.
- **L-Tyrosine** -- Same issue as NAC. Amino acids in food compete for transport across the blood-brain barrier.
- **Probiotics** -- Most strains survive better in an empty stomach with lower acid levels. Take first thing in the morning with water.
- **Collagen peptides** -- Better absorbed without competing proteins. Take in the morning with water or coffee.

sections.heading: Must Take With Food
sections.body:
These need fat or food for absorption:

- **Vitamin D3** -- Fat-soluble. Taking with a fatty meal increases absorption by 32-50%.
- **Vitamin K2** -- Fat-soluble. Same principle as D3. Take them together with food.
- **Omega-3 / Fish Oil** -- Fat-soluble and dramatically better absorbed with a meal. Also reduces fishy burps.
- **CoQ10 (ubiquinol)** -- Fat-soluble. Up to 3x better absorption with food containing fat.
- **Curcumin/Turmeric** -- Poorly absorbed on its own. Fat and black pepper both help significantly.
- **Vitamin E** -- Fat-soluble. Needs dietary fat for proper absorption.
- **Ashwagandha** -- Fat improves absorption of the active withanolides.

sections.heading: Doesn't Matter Either Way
sections.body:
These work fine with or without food:

- **Magnesium** -- All forms absorb well regardless of food timing. Some people get stomach upset on an empty stomach, so take with food if that's you.
- **Creatine** -- Absorbs fine either way. Daily consistency matters more than timing.
- **B vitamins** -- Water-soluble, absorb well regardless. Some people feel nauseous taking B vitamins on an empty stomach.
- **Vitamin C** -- Water-soluble. Food doesn't significantly affect absorption.
- **Zinc** -- Technically absorbs slightly better on an empty stomach, but often causes nausea. Taking with food is a reasonable trade-off.
- **L-Theanine** -- Works fast either way.

sections.heading: Quick Reference Table
sections.body:
| Empty Stomach | With Food | Either Way |
|---|---|---|
| Iron | Vitamin D3 | Magnesium |
| NAC | Vitamin K2 | Creatine |
| L-Tyrosine | Omega-3 | B Vitamins |
| Probiotics | CoQ10 | Vitamin C |
| Collagen | Curcumin | L-Theanine |

Use our [Supplement Checker](/) to get personalized timing recommendations for your full stack.

title: Does Ashwagandha Lower or Raise Testosterone?
metaDescription: Ashwagandha has been shown to increase testosterone by 14-40% in studies. Here's what the research says about dosage, timeline, and who benefits most.
intro: There's confusion online about whether ashwagandha helps or hurts testosterone. The research is actually quite clear -- but the answer depends on your starting point.

sections.heading: What the Studies Show
sections.body:
Multiple controlled studies consistently show ashwagandha **increases** testosterone:

- **Stressed adults:** A 2019 study found 600mg KSM-66 daily increased testosterone by 14.7% over 8 weeks, primarily by reducing cortisol (which suppresses testosterone production).
- **Infertile men:** A 2010 study showed a 10-22% increase in testosterone across three months. Sperm quality also improved significantly.
- **Resistance-trained men:** A 2015 study found 600mg/day for 8 weeks resulted in significantly greater testosterone increases compared to placebo, alongside better strength gains.

No published study has shown ashwagandha lowering testosterone.

sections.heading: Why the Confusion Exists
sections.body:
The "ashwagandha lowers testosterone" myth likely comes from two things:

**1. It lowers cortisol.** Some people confuse cortisol with testosterone. Ashwagandha reduces cortisol by 25-30%. Since high cortisol suppresses testosterone, this is actually a mechanism by which ashwagandha *supports* testosterone.

**2. It can lower thyroid stimulation.** In people with hyperthyroidism, ashwagandha may normalize thyroid hormones. Some online sources conflated this with a testosterone effect, which is incorrect.

sections.heading: Who Benefits Most
sections.body:
The testosterone boost is most significant in:

- **Stressed individuals** -- If your cortisol is chronically elevated (poor sleep, high stress job, overtraining), you'll see the biggest benefit because cortisol directly suppresses testosterone.
- **Men with suboptimal levels** -- If your testosterone is already optimal, the effect is smaller.
- **Older adults** -- Age-related cortisol increases make ashwagandha more impactful.

If you're a healthy 25-year-old with low stress and good sleep, don't expect dramatic testosterone changes. Ashwagandha works primarily by removing cortisol's brake on testosterone production.

sections.heading: How to Take It
sections.body:
- **Form:** KSM-66 extract (most studied) or Sensoril
- **Dose:** 300-600mg daily
- **Timing:** Evening with dinner (fat improves absorption, and it supports sleep)
- **Timeline:** Effects start around week 4, peak at week 8-12
- **Cycling:** Consider 8 weeks on, 2-4 weeks off to maintain sensitivity

Ashwagandha pairs well with [magnesium](/stack/ashwagandha-magnesium) for stress and sleep. Check our [Supplement Checker](/) for interactions with your full stack.

title: Should You Take Creatine on Rest Days?
metaDescription: Yes, you should take creatine on rest days. Here's why daily dosing matters more than timing and what happens if you skip days.
intro: Short answer: yes. Creatine works through saturation, not acute dosing. Here's why skipping rest days undermines the whole point.

sections.heading: How Creatine Actually Works
sections.body:
Creatine isn't like caffeine -- you don't take it and feel an effect 30 minutes later. It works by saturating your muscle cells over time.

Your muscles store creatine phosphate, which rapidly regenerates ATP (your cells' energy currency) during high-intensity effort. When your stores are full, you can push out 1-2 extra reps, recover faster between sets, and produce more power.

It takes 2-4 weeks of daily dosing (5g/day) to fully saturate your muscles. Skipping rest days slows down saturation and can let levels start to drop.

sections.heading: What Happens If You Skip Days
sections.body:
Your muscle creatine levels decrease by about 1.7% per day when you stop supplementing. That means:

- **Skip 1 day:** Negligible effect
- **Skip a week:** Noticeable decline in stored creatine
- **Skip 2+ weeks:** You're losing most of your saturation and will need to rebuild

The convenience of 5g daily (about one teaspoon) is hard to argue against. Just mix it with water, coffee, or a shake and move on.

sections.heading: Rest Day Dosing Protocol
sections.body:
**On training days:** 5g any time. Pre-workout, post-workout, with breakfast -- doesn't matter.

**On rest days:** 5g any time. With your morning coffee, with lunch, whenever you remember.

The timing is genuinely irrelevant. Consistency is the only thing that matters with creatine. Set a daily reminder if you tend to forget.

No loading phase needed. 5g/day reaches full saturation in 3-4 weeks. Loading (20g/day for a week) gets you there faster but often causes bloating and stomach issues.

sections.heading: Common Creatine Myths
sections.body:
- **"Creatine causes hair loss"** -- Based on one small study that showed increased DHT. Never replicated. The evidence is extremely weak.
- **"Creatine is bad for your kidneys"** -- Debunked in dozens of studies on healthy individuals. Only a concern for people with pre-existing kidney disease.
- **"You need to cycle creatine"** -- No. There's no tolerance buildup. Daily use is safe long-term.
- **"Creatine is a steroid"** -- It's an amino acid compound naturally found in meat. It's one of the safest supplements studied.

Curious how creatine interacts with your other supplements? Run your stack through the [Supplement Checker](/).

title: Why Does Fish Oil Make You Burp (And How to Stop It)
metaDescription: Fish oil burps happen because of oxidation and poor digestion. Here's how to stop fishy burps with simple changes to when and how you take your fish oil.
intro: The dreaded fish oil burp. It's the #1 reason people quit taking omega-3s. But it's completely avoidable once you understand why it happens.

sections.heading: Why It Happens
sections.body:
Fish oil burps are caused by two things:

**1. Rancid or oxidized oil.** This is the most common cause. Fish oil is highly prone to oxidation (going rancid). Once oxidized, it produces trimethylamine and other compounds that taste and smell like low tide. Many cheap fish oils are already partially oxidized before you buy them.

**2. Slow digestion.** When fish oil sits in your stomach too long (especially on an empty stomach), the capsule dissolves and oil rises to the top of your stomach contents. Every time you burp, you taste it.

sections.heading: How to Stop the Burps
sections.body:
**Switch brands.** If your current fish oil gives you burps, it's likely oxidized. Quality indicators:
- Look for IFOS (International Fish Oil Standards) certification
- Check the packaging date -- buy the freshest available
- Bite into a capsule. It should taste mildly fishy, not rancid or strongly unpleasant
- Store in the refrigerator after opening

**Take with food.** Always take fish oil with a full meal, ideally your largest meal of the day. The food helps emulsify the oil and speeds digestion so it doesn't sit in your stomach.

**Freeze the capsules.** This is the classic hack. Frozen capsules dissolve much slower, so they pass through your stomach and dissolve in your intestines instead. No stomach contact = no burps.

**Try liquid fish oil.** Sounds counterintuitive, but high-quality liquid fish oil (like Nordic Naturals) often causes fewer burps because you mix it with food directly.

sections.heading: Consider Switching Forms
sections.body:
If nothing helps, try these alternatives:

- **Algae-based omega-3** -- Derived from the same algae fish eat. No fish taste at all. Good option for people who can't tolerate fish oil.
- **Krill oil** -- Phospholipid-bound omega-3s that are more water-soluble. Causes significantly fewer digestive issues. More expensive per gram of EPA/DHA.
- **Eat more fatty fish** -- Salmon, mackerel, sardines 2-3x per week can replace supplementation entirely.

Omega-3 pairs well with [vitamin D](/stack/omega-3-vitamin-d) and other fat-soluble supplements. Use our [Supplement Checker](/) to optimize your stack.

title: Can You Take Supplements With Coffee?
metaDescription: Some supplements are fine with coffee, others get blocked by caffeine or tannins. Here's which supplements you can and can't take with your morning cup.
intro: Most people take their morning supplements with coffee. For some supplements that's perfectly fine. For others, you're flushing money down the drain. Here's the breakdown.

sections.heading: Safe to Take With Coffee
sections.body:
These supplements are unaffected by coffee or actually benefit from it:

- **Creatine** -- Dissolves fine in coffee and absorption is unaffected. A popular combo.
- **L-Theanine** -- The classic caffeine + theanine pairing. Theanine smooths out coffee jitters. This is intentional, not accidental.
- **Vitamin D3** -- Coffee doesn't significantly affect absorption. Just make sure you have some fat with it (cream or a breakfast).
- **Collagen peptides** -- Many people stir collagen into coffee. No absorption issues.
- **B vitamins** -- Water-soluble and unaffected by coffee. Just note that both are stimulating, so don't blame the B vitamins if you feel jittery.

sections.heading: Do NOT Take With Coffee
sections.body:
Coffee contains tannins, caffeine, and chlorogenic acid that interfere with these:

- **Iron** -- Coffee reduces iron absorption by up to 80%. This is the biggest one. Wait at least 1 hour after coffee before taking iron (or take iron at a different time entirely).
- **Calcium** -- Caffeine increases calcium excretion through urine. Not a huge effect at moderate coffee intake, but don't wash down your calcium supplement with coffee.
- **Zinc** -- Tannins in coffee can bind to zinc and reduce absorption. Space by 1 hour.
- **Thyroid medication (levothyroxine)** -- Not a supplement, but important. Coffee drastically reduces absorption. Take thyroid meds 60 minutes before coffee.

sections.heading: Timing Matters More Than You Think
sections.body:
Coffee's interference effects last about 1 hour. The simplest strategy:

**With morning coffee:** Creatine, L-theanine, vitamin D, B vitamins, collagen

**1+ hour after coffee:** Iron, zinc, calcium, thyroid meds

**Evening (no coffee conflict):** Magnesium, ashwagandha, glycine, melatonin

This naturally splits your supplements into a morning coffee stack and an evening relaxation stack, which is good practice anyway.

sections.heading: What About Tea?
sections.body:
Green tea and black tea have the same tannin issue as coffee -- avoid taking iron and zinc with tea as well. Green tea extract (EGCG) is actually one of the worst offenders for blocking iron absorption.

Herbal teas (chamomile, peppermint, rooibos) are generally fine with all supplements.

Want to check if your specific stack has any conflicts? Use the [Supplement Checker](/) for a full interaction analysis.

title: Do You Need to Cycle Ashwagandha?
metaDescription: Should you take ashwagandha continuously or cycle it? Here's what the research says about tolerance, long-term safety, and optimal cycling protocols.
intro: Ashwagandha is one of the most popular adaptogens, but there's a lot of debate about whether you need to cycle it. Some people take it daily for years. Others do 8 weeks on, 2 weeks off. Here's what actually makes sense.

sections.heading: What the Research Says
sections.body:
Most clinical studies on ashwagandha run for 8-12 weeks of continuous use. In these studies:

- Benefits peak around week 8 (cortisol reduction, testosterone increase, anxiety reduction)
- No tolerance buildup was observed within the study period
- Side effects remained minimal throughout
- No withdrawal effects were reported when stopping

The honest answer is that we don't have many long-term studies (6+ months of continuous use). Most of the cycling advice comes from traditional Ayurvedic practice and anecdotal reports, not clinical data.

sections.heading: Why People Recommend Cycling
sections.body:
The arguments for cycling:

**1. Adaptogen theory.** Traditional practice holds that adaptogens work best when cycled because your body can adapt to their effects over time. This is plausible but not well-studied.

**2. Thyroid effects.** Ashwagandha can increase thyroid hormone levels (T3 and T4). For people with normal thyroid function, long-term continuous use could theoretically push levels too high. This is the strongest argument for cycling.

**3. Anecdotal "diminishing returns."** Some users report that ashwagandha becomes less effective after 2-3 months of continuous use. Taking a break seems to "reset" its effectiveness.

sections.heading: Recommended Protocol
sections.body:
Based on the available evidence and common practice:

**Conservative approach (recommended):**
- 8 weeks on, 2-4 weeks off
- This aligns with study durations and allows any thyroid effects to normalize

**Moderate approach:**
- 12 weeks on, 4 weeks off
- Allows benefits to fully accumulate before resetting

**Situational use:**
- Only take it during high-stress periods (deadlines, travel, difficult life events)
- Stop when the stressor passes
- This may actually be the most practical approach for many people

sections.heading: Who Should Definitely Cycle
sections.body:
- **Anyone with thyroid issues** -- Monitor thyroid levels and cycle conservatively
- **People on thyroid medication** -- Ashwagandha can alter your medication needs. Consult your doctor.
- **Anyone who notices emotional blunting** -- Some people report feeling "too calm" or emotionally flat after prolonged use. A break usually resolves this.

Ashwagandha stacks well with [magnesium](/stack/ashwagandha-magnesium) for stress and sleep. Run your full stack through the [Supplement Checker](/) to flag any interactions.

title: Magnesium Glycinate vs Citrate vs Threonate: Which Form Is Best?
metaDescription: The form of magnesium matters more than the dose. Compare glycinate, citrate, threonate, and other forms for sleep, anxiety, cramps, and brain health.
intro: There are at least 10 forms of magnesium supplements and they're not interchangeable. Each form has different bioavailability, different effects, and different best uses. Here's the no-nonsense comparison.

sections.heading: Magnesium Glycinate -- Best All-Around
sections.body:
**Best for:** Sleep, anxiety, general supplementation

**Why:** High bioavailability (well absorbed), and the glycinate (glycine) component is itself a calming neurotransmitter. You're getting two sleep/relaxation supplements in one. Gentle on the stomach -- rarely causes digestive issues.

**Dose:** 200-400mg elemental magnesium before bed

**Downside:** More expensive than citrate. Capsules are large because glycinate molecules are bulky (often 2-3 capsules per dose).

sections.heading: Magnesium Citrate -- Best for Absorption + Gut Health
sections.body:
**Best for:** General supplementation, constipation relief

**Why:** One of the most bioavailable forms. Citrate has a mild osmotic effect that draws water into the intestines, which makes it useful for people with constipation but a drawback for those with sensitive stomachs.

**Dose:** 200-400mg elemental magnesium

**Downside:** Can cause loose stools at higher doses. Not as calming as glycinate for sleep.

sections.heading: Magnesium Threonate (Magtein) -- Best for Brain
sections.body:
**Best for:** Cognitive function, memory, brain fog

**Why:** The only form shown to significantly cross the blood-brain barrier and increase brain magnesium levels. A 2010 MIT study showed it enhanced learning, working memory, and both short and long-term memory in animal models.

**Dose:** 1-2g magnesium threonate (contains ~144mg elemental magnesium per gram)

**Downside:** Expensive. Low elemental magnesium per capsule means you may need to supplement with another form for total daily intake. The human evidence is still limited.

sections.heading: Other Forms Worth Knowing
sections.body:
- **Magnesium Malate** -- Paired with malic acid (involved in energy production). Some people prefer this for daytime use and exercise recovery. Less sedating than glycinate.
- **Magnesium Taurate** -- Combined with taurine, which supports cardiovascular health. Often recommended for heart health and blood pressure.
- **Magnesium Oxide** -- Cheap but only 4% bioavailability. Mostly acts as a laxative. Not recommended for supplementing magnesium levels.
- **Magnesium L-Threonate** -- Same as threonate above (Magtein is the branded version).
- **Magnesium Chloride** -- Used topically in sprays and baths. Absorbs through skin. Evidence for transdermal absorption is mixed.

sections.heading: Which Should You Buy?
sections.body:
| Goal | Best Form |
|---|---|
| Sleep | Glycinate |
| Anxiety/Stress | Glycinate |
| Constipation | Citrate |
| General deficiency | Citrate or Glycinate |
| Brain health/memory | Threonate |
| Exercise recovery | Malate |
| Heart health | Taurate |

**Budget pick:** Magnesium citrate from NOW Foods (~$8/month)
**Premium pick:** Magnesium glycinate from Thorne (~$15/month)
**Brain-focused:** Magtein magnesium threonate (~$25/month)

Magnesium interacts with several other supplements. Check our [Supplement Checker](/) to see how it fits with your stack.

title: What Supplements to Take in the Morning vs Night
metaDescription: The complete morning vs evening supplement timing guide. Which supplements are energizing, which are sedating, and the optimal split for your stack.
intro: Timing your supplements correctly can be the difference between a stack that works and one that actively works against you. Taking magnesium in the morning can make you drowsy. Taking B vitamins at night can keep you up. Here's how to split your stack.

sections.heading: Morning Supplements
sections.body:
These are energizing, need morning sunlight/food, or interfere with sleep:

- **Vitamin D3** -- Mimics sunlight signaling. Some evidence it can suppress melatonin if taken at night.
- **B Complex** -- Energizing. B6 and B12 can interfere with sleep if taken in the evening.
- **Iron** -- Best absorbed on an empty stomach first thing in the morning. Also energizing for deficient individuals.
- **CoQ10** -- Supports cellular energy production. Can be mildly stimulating.
- **Caffeine + L-Theanine** -- Obviously. Cut off caffeine by 2pm.
- **Tyrosine** -- Dopamine precursor. Stimulating. Take on an empty stomach in the morning.
- **Rhodiola** -- Adaptogen that combats fatigue. Mildly stimulating. Morning only.

sections.heading: Evening Supplements
sections.body:
These are calming, sedating, or work best during sleep:

- **Magnesium (glycinate)** -- Activates GABA, relaxes muscles. Take 30-60 min before bed.
- **Glycine** -- Lowers core body temperature, enhances sleep quality. 3g before bed.
- **Ashwagandha** -- Reduces cortisol. Evening with dinner supports wind-down.
- **Melatonin** -- Obviously. 0.3-0.5mg, 30 min before target sleep time.
- **L-Theanine** -- Works both morning (with caffeine) and evening (for sleep). Versatile.
- **Apigenin** -- Mild GABA agonist. 50mg before bed.
- **Zinc** -- Mildly sedating for some people. Evening with dinner.

sections.heading: Anytime Supplements
sections.body:
These don't have significant energizing or sedating effects:

- **Creatine** -- Daily consistency matters, timing doesn't. Pick a time and stick with it.
- **Omega-3** -- Take with your fattiest meal, morning or evening.
- **Vitamin K2** -- Take with D3 and fat. Usually morning.
- **NAC** -- Empty stomach. Morning or evening, 30 min before a meal.
- **Probiotics** -- Morning on empty stomach is slightly preferred.
- **Curcumin/Turmeric** -- With any meal containing fat and black pepper.

sections.heading: Sample Daily Schedule
sections.body:
**Morning (with breakfast):**
- Vitamin D3 + K2
- B Complex
- Omega-3
- Creatine
- Caffeine + L-Theanine

**Empty stomach (30 min before breakfast):**
- Iron (if supplementing)
- Probiotics
- NAC

**Evening (with dinner):**
- Ashwagandha
- Zinc
- Curcumin

**Before bed:**
- Magnesium glycinate
- Glycine
- Apigenin

Want a personalized timing schedule? Enter your supplements in the [Supplement Checker](/) -- it generates morning and evening splits automatically.

title: Is Creatine Safe for Teenagers?
metaDescription: Can teens take creatine? Here's what the research says about creatine safety for adolescents, recommended doses, and what parents should know.
intro: Creatine is the most studied supplement in sports nutrition history, but most of that research is on adults. If you're a teenager (or a parent of one), here's what the evidence actually says.

sections.heading: What the Research Shows
sections.body:
The International Society of Sports Nutrition (ISSN) published a position stand in 2017 stating:

- Creatine is one of the most well-studied and safest supplements available
- Short and long-term supplementation (up to 5 years) is safe in healthy individuals
- There is no compelling evidence that creatine is unsafe for adolescents

However, they note that most studies have been conducted on adults aged 18+. The limited studies on adolescents (ages 13-18) have shown similar safety profiles with no adverse effects reported.

The American Academy of Pediatrics takes a more conservative stance, noting that supplements in general are not well-regulated and recommending food-first approaches.

sections.heading: Why the Controversy
sections.body:
The concerns about creatine for teens are mostly theoretical:

**"It's bad for kidneys"** -- Debunked in dozens of studies on adults. Creatine raises creatinine levels (a kidney waste marker), which can look concerning on blood tests but doesn't indicate kidney damage in healthy people.

**"Teenagers are still developing"** -- True, but creatine is a natural compound found in meat and fish. Your body already produces 1-2g daily. Supplementing adds 3-5g on top of that.

**"It's a gateway to steroids"** -- This is a social concern, not a medical one. Creatine is not a hormone and has zero resemblance to anabolic steroids in mechanism or effect.

sections.heading: Practical Recommendations for Teens
sections.body:
If a teenager decides to take creatine:

- **Dose:** 3-5g creatine monohydrate daily (same as adults). No loading phase needed.
- **Form:** Plain creatine monohydrate. Avoid proprietary blends or "advanced" forms -- they're more expensive and no more effective.
- **Hydration:** Drink plenty of water. Creatine pulls water into muscles, so hydration is important.
- **Skip if:** The teen has any kidney condition, is taking nephrotoxic medications, or hasn't consulted a doctor.
- **Priorities first:** Creatine is the cherry on top. Proper training, nutrition, sleep, and hydration should come first. No supplement compensates for poor fundamentals.

sections.heading: What Parents Should Know
sections.body:
- Creatine is not a steroid, not banned in any sport (including NCAA), and is naturally present in food
- The biggest risk with teen supplementation is unregulated products containing contaminants -- buy from brands with third-party testing (NSF Certified for Sport)
- A 5g daily dose of creatine monohydrate is equivalent to eating about 2 pounds of red meat
- If you're uncomfortable with supplementation, eating more meat and fish naturally increases creatine intake

Want to check if creatine interacts with other supplements? Use our [Supplement Checker](/) for a free interaction check.

title: Which Supplements to Stop Before Surgery
metaDescription: Some supplements increase bleeding risk or interact with anesthesia. Here's what to stop 1-2 weeks before surgery and which ones are safe to continue.
intro: Your surgeon probably told you to stop supplements before surgery, but may not have specified which ones. Some supplements increase bleeding risk, interfere with anesthesia, or affect blood pressure. Here's the complete list.

sections.heading: Stop 2 Weeks Before Surgery
sections.body:
These have blood-thinning or cardiovascular effects that can cause complications:

- **Fish Oil / Omega-3** -- Inhibits platelet aggregation (blood clotting). One of the most important to stop.
- **Vitamin E** -- Blood-thinning effect at doses above 400 IU.
- **Ginkgo Biloba** -- Significant antiplatelet activity. Multiple case reports of surgical bleeding.
- **Garlic supplements** -- Inhibits platelet aggregation. Raw garlic in food is fine, concentrated supplements are not.
- **Turmeric/Curcumin** -- Anti-inflammatory and mild blood-thinning properties.
- **Ginger supplements** -- High-dose ginger can affect clotting. Ginger in food is fine.
- **Green Tea Extract** -- Can affect blood clotting and may interfere with certain anesthetics.

sections.heading: Stop 1 Week Before Surgery
sections.body:
These can interact with anesthesia or affect recovery:

- **St. John's Wort** -- Interacts with a huge number of medications including anesthetics. One of the most dangerous supplements to combine with surgery.
- **Valerian** -- Can amplify the effects of anesthesia and sedatives.
- **Kava** -- Liver concerns and sedative interaction with anesthesia.
- **Ephedra/Ma Huang** -- If anyone still takes this. Cardiovascular risks under anesthesia.
- **Ashwagandha** -- May amplify sedative effects of anesthesia. Limited data but worth stopping.

sections.heading: Generally Safe to Continue
sections.body:
These don't have significant surgical risks:

- **Vitamin D** -- No surgical concerns at normal doses
- **Magnesium** -- Fine to continue. May actually help with post-surgical muscle relaxation
- **Probiotics** -- No surgical interaction
- **B vitamins** -- Safe to continue
- **Creatine** -- No known surgical interactions
- **Vitamin C** -- Fine at normal doses. Some surgeons even recommend it for wound healing

**Important:** Always give your surgeon and anesthesiologist a complete list of everything you take. When in doubt, stop it.

sections.heading: After Surgery
sections.body:
Wait for your surgeon's clearance before restarting blood-thinning supplements. Most people can resume everything 1-2 weeks post-surgery once healing is confirmed.

Supplements that may help recovery (ask your doctor first):
- **Vitamin C** -- Supports collagen synthesis and wound healing
- **Zinc** -- Involved in tissue repair
- **Protein/Collagen** -- Building blocks for tissue rebuilding
- **Probiotics** -- Antibiotics from surgery disrupt gut flora

Use the [Supplement Checker](/) to verify your post-surgery stack has no conflicts.

title: How Long Does Creatine Take to Work?
metaDescription: Creatine takes 2-4 weeks to fully saturate muscles at 5g/day. Here's the exact timeline, what to expect, and why loading phases are usually unnecessary.
intro: You started taking creatine and feel nothing after a week. That's normal. Creatine works through saturation, not acute effects. Here's the exact timeline.

sections.heading: The Timeline
sections.body:
**Without loading (5g/day):**
- **Week 1:** Muscle creatine levels start rising. No noticeable performance effect yet.
- **Week 2:** Levels at roughly 60-70% saturation. Some people start noticing better performance in high-rep sets.
- **Week 3-4:** Full saturation reached. This is when you'll notice the real difference -- more reps, faster recovery between sets, better power output.
- **Ongoing:** Maintain with 5g/day. Effects persist as long as you keep taking it.

**With loading (20g/day for 5-7 days, then 5g/day):**
- **Day 5-7:** Full saturation reached. Faster results but often causes bloating, water retention, and stomach discomfort.
- Loading is unnecessary for most people. You end up at the same place -- it just takes 2-3 weeks longer without loading.

sections.heading: What You'll Actually Notice
sections.body:
Creatine isn't like caffeine -- you won't "feel" it kick in. The effects are subtle but measurable:

- **1-2 extra reps** on heavy sets (this is the most consistent effect)
- **Faster recovery** between sets (less fatigue on set 3, 4, 5)
- **Slight weight gain** of 2-4 lbs from water retention in muscles (this is normal and desirable -- it's intramuscular water, not bloating)
- **Better performance** in high-intensity, short-duration efforts (sprints, heavy lifts, HIIT)

You won't notice effects in endurance activities like long-distance running. Creatine is for the ATP-PC energy system (efforts under 30 seconds).

sections.heading: What If It's Not Working?
sections.body:
About 20-30% of people are "non-responders" to creatine. This is usually because:

- **High meat diet:** If you eat a lot of red meat, your muscle creatine stores may already be near-full. Supplementation has less room to add benefit.
- **Insufficient dose:** Make sure you're taking 5g daily, not 2-3g. Under-dosing is common.
- **Inconsistency:** Skipping days prevents full saturation. Daily dosing is essential.
- **Wrong expectations:** Creatine adds 5-10% to your performance ceiling. If you're expecting dramatic changes, recalibrate.

Vegetarians and vegans tend to be the best responders because their baseline muscle creatine levels are lower.

title: Can You Take Too Much Vitamin D? Signs of Toxicity
metaDescription: Vitamin D toxicity is rare but real. Learn the symptoms, the actual dangerous dose, and how to supplement safely without overdoing it.
intro: Vitamin D is one of the most important supplements, but it's also one of the few where toxicity is possible. The good news: you'd have to try pretty hard to overdose. Here's what you need to know.

sections.heading: How Much Is Too Much
sections.body:
**Safe daily range:** 2,000-4,000 IU for most adults

**Upper tolerable limit (per IOM):** 4,000 IU/day

**Where toxicity starts:** Generally above 10,000 IU/day taken consistently for months, OR blood levels above 150 ng/mL.

**Important context:** Most vitamin D toxicity cases in medical literature involved people taking 50,000-100,000 IU daily for extended periods. At 2,000-5,000 IU/day, toxicity is essentially impossible in healthy adults.

You cannot get vitamin D toxicity from sun exposure -- your body self-regulates production.

sections.heading: Signs of Vitamin D Toxicity
sections.body:
Vitamin D toxicity causes hypercalcemia (too much calcium in the blood). Symptoms include:

**Early signs:**
- Nausea and vomiting
- Poor appetite
- Constipation
- Excessive thirst and frequent urination
- Weakness and fatigue

**Severe (prolonged overdose):**
- Kidney stones
- Kidney damage
- Bone pain
- Confusion and disorientation
- Heart rhythm abnormalities

If you experience these symptoms and are taking high-dose vitamin D, stop immediately and see a doctor. A simple blood test (25-hydroxyvitamin D) confirms whether levels are dangerously high.

sections.heading: Why K2 Is Your Safety Net
sections.body:
Most of vitamin D's toxicity comes from calcium being deposited in the wrong places (arteries, kidneys) instead of bones.

**Vitamin K2 (MK-7)** activates proteins that direct calcium into bones and teeth. It's essentially a safety mechanism against the calcium misrouting that high vitamin D can cause.

This is why we always recommend pairing D3 with K2. Check our [Vitamin D + K2 page](/stack/vitamin-d-k2) for the full breakdown.

**Rule of thumb:** 100-200mcg K2 (MK-7) per 2,000-5,000 IU D3.

sections.heading: How to Supplement Safely
sections.body:
- **Test your blood levels** before starting high doses. A 25-hydroxyvitamin D test costs $30-50.
- **Target 40-60 ng/mL** -- this is the optimal range per most functional medicine practitioners.
- **Don't mega-dose** without medical supervision. 2,000-5,000 IU daily is sufficient for most people.
- **Always pair with K2** to ensure proper calcium routing.
- **Take with fat** for better absorption.
- **Retest every 6-12 months** if taking more than 2,000 IU daily.

Check how vitamin D interacts with your full stack using the [Supplement Checker](/).

title: When Is the Best Time to Take Probiotics?
metaDescription: Should you take probiotics on an empty stomach or with food? Morning or night? Here's what the research says about optimal probiotic timing.
intro: The timing advice for probiotics is all over the place. Some brands say with food, others say empty stomach. Here's what the research actually shows.

sections.heading: The Short Answer
sections.body:
Take probiotics **on an empty stomach, first thing in the morning**, 30 minutes before eating.

A 2011 study published in Beneficial Microbes tested probiotic survival under different conditions and found that survival was best when taken 30 minutes before a meal or with a meal containing fat. Survival was worst when taken 30 minutes after a meal.

The reasoning: your stomach acid is at its lowest levels when your stomach is empty (especially first thing in the morning). This gives the bacteria the best chance of surviving the journey to your intestines.

sections.heading: Does the Strain Matter?
sections.body:
Yes. Different strains have different acid tolerance:

- **Lactobacillus and Bifidobacterium** -- More acid-sensitive. Benefit most from empty stomach timing.
- **Saccharomyces boulardii** -- A yeast-based probiotic that's acid-resistant. Timing doesn't matter much.
- **Spore-based probiotics (Bacillus strains)** -- Naturally protected by spore coating. Very acid-resistant. Can be taken any time.

If your probiotic is enteric-coated (designed to survive stomach acid), timing matters less since the coating protects the bacteria regardless.

sections.heading: Morning vs Night
sections.body:
**Morning is generally preferred** because:

- Stomach acid is lowest after the overnight fast
- You're less likely to forget
- The bacteria have all day to colonize before your next meal disrupts the gut environment

**Evening can work** if you take it 2-3 hours after dinner before bed. Your stomach will be relatively empty by then.

The most important factor is **consistency** -- taking probiotics at the same time daily matters more than the specific time you choose.

sections.heading: What to Avoid Taking With Probiotics
sections.body:
- **Antibiotics** -- They kill probiotics. If you're on antibiotics, take probiotics at least 2 hours apart (and continue probiotics for 2 weeks after finishing the antibiotic course).
- **Hot beverages** -- Heat kills bacteria. Don't mix probiotics into hot coffee or tea.
- **Alcohol** -- Disrupts gut flora and can kill probiotic bacteria.

**Good to combine with:**
- **Prebiotics (fiber)** -- Feed the probiotics. Many supplements include both (called synbiotics).
- **Cold water** -- The simplest and best vehicle.

Check how probiotics fit with your full supplement stack using the [Supplement Checker](/).

title: Does Melatonin Stop Working? Tolerance and Alternatives
metaDescription: Melatonin can lose effectiveness over time. Here's why it happens, whether you build tolerance, and better alternatives for long-term sleep.
intro: You started with 5mg of melatonin and it worked great. Then you needed 10mg. Now even that doesn't help. Sound familiar? Here's what's happening and what to do about it.

sections.heading: Do You Build Tolerance?
sections.body:
Technically, melatonin doesn't cause classical pharmacological tolerance the way sleeping pills do. Your melatonin receptors don't downregulate significantly.

But many people report it becoming less effective over time. The likely reasons:

**1. You're taking too much.** The physiological dose of melatonin is 0.3-0.5mg. Most supplements sell 5-10mg -- that's 10-30x the natural dose. At these levels, you're flooding your receptors and disrupting your natural melatonin cycle.

**2. Timing drift.** Melatonin works best when taken at a consistent time. If your schedule is irregular, the signal gets noisy.

**3. The placebo wore off.** Some of melatonin's initial effectiveness is placebo. Once that fades, you're left with the actual (more modest) pharmacological effect.

sections.heading: The Dose Problem
sections.body:
This is the most common issue. Here's what the research shows:

- **0.3mg** -- The MIT-researched physiological dose. Raises blood melatonin to natural nighttime levels.
- **0.5mg** -- Slightly above physiological. Still reasonable.
- **1mg** -- Starting to exceed natural levels significantly.
- **3-5mg** -- Raises melatonin to 10-20x natural levels. Can cause next-day grogginess.
- **10mg+** -- Way too much for most people. Can paradoxically worsen sleep by disrupting the natural rhythm.

**The fix:** Drop down to 0.3-0.5mg. You might sleep better on less. Many people who "stopped responding" to 10mg find that 0.3mg works because it mimics the natural signal instead of overwhelming it.

sections.heading: Better Long-Term Sleep Supplements
sections.body:
Melatonin is a signal -- it tells your body it's nighttime. It's not a sedative. For actual sleep quality improvement, these work better long-term:

- **Magnesium glycinate (400mg)** -- GABA activation + muscle relaxation. No tolerance issues.
- **Glycine (3g)** -- Lowers core body temperature. Improves sleep quality in studies. No tolerance.
- **L-Theanine (200mg)** -- Promotes alpha brain waves and calm. Safe for daily long-term use.
- **Apigenin (50mg)** -- Mild GABA agonist from chamomile. No tolerance reported.

These work through different mechanisms than melatonin and don't lose effectiveness. See our full [Sleep Stack guide](/blog/best-supplement-stack-for-sleep).

sections.heading: When Melatonin Makes Sense
sections.body:
Melatonin is best used situationally, not daily:

- **Jet lag** -- Take 0.5mg at bedtime in your destination time zone. This is melatonin's strongest use case.
- **Shift work** -- Helps reset your circadian rhythm when your schedule changes.
- **Occasional insomnia** -- 0.3-0.5mg when you need extra help falling asleep.
- **Phase shifting** -- If you want to move your bedtime earlier, take 0.3mg about 2 hours before your target bedtime.

For nightly use, the supplements above are more sustainable. Run your sleep stack through the [Supplement Checker](/) to make sure everything works together.

title: Do Gummy Vitamins Actually Work?
metaDescription: Gummy vitamins are convenient but often contain less active ingredients, more sugar, and degrade faster than capsules. Here's the honest truth.
intro: Gummy vitamins are a $6 billion market. They taste great and they're easy to take. But are they actually delivering what they promise? The answer is complicated.

sections.heading: The Problems With Gummies
sections.body:
**1. Lower doses.** Gummy formulations can't hold as much active ingredient as capsules. Manufacturers compensate by requiring 2-3 gummies per serving, but even then, doses are often lower than capsule equivalents.

**2. Stability issues.** Active ingredients degrade faster in gummy format. A 2017 ConsumerLab analysis found that many gummy vitamins contained significantly less of the labeled nutrients than claimed -- sometimes less than 50%.

**3. Added sugar.** Most gummies contain 2-4g of sugar per serving. If you're taking multiple gummy supplements, that adds up to a meaningful amount of daily sugar.

**4. Fewer nutrients.** Some vitamins and minerals (iron, calcium, magnesium) are difficult to formulate as gummies due to taste or stability. Your gummy multivitamin is almost certainly missing key minerals.

sections.heading: When Gummies Are Fine
sections.body:
Gummies are better than nothing. If the choice is between a gummy vitamin you'll actually take and a capsule that sits in your cabinet, take the gummy.

They're reasonable for:

- **Kids** who won't swallow pills
- **Vitamin D** -- Simple molecule, stable enough in gummy form
- **Vitamin C** -- Water-soluble, relatively stable
- **B12** -- Effective in gummy form for most people

For these simple, single-ingredient supplements, gummies deliver adequate amounts.

sections.heading: When to Use Capsules or Softgels Instead
sections.body:
Switch to capsules/softgels for:

- **Magnesium** -- Gummies can't hold a meaningful dose. You'd need 6+ gummies to get 400mg.
- **Fish oil / Omega-3** -- Softgels protect the oil from oxidation. Gummy omega-3s often have minimal EPA/DHA.
- **Iron** -- Rarely available as a gummy and degrades quickly in that format.
- **Multivitamins** -- Capsule multis consistently outperform gummy multis in testing.
- **Any supplement where dose precision matters** -- Gummies have wider manufacturing variance.

**The bottom line:** Gummies are a convenience trade-off. You're getting less potency and paying more per dose for a better taste experience. For your core supplements, capsules and softgels are worth the switch.

sections.heading: How to Check Your Gummy Quality
sections.body:
If you prefer gummies, minimize the downsides:

- **Check third-party testing** -- NSF or USP certified gummies are verified to contain what they claim.
- **Look at the "Other Ingredients"** -- Less sugar, fewer artificial colors, no high-fructose corn syrup.
- **Compare doses to capsule equivalents** -- If the gummy has 50% of the dose a capsule would, you're paying more for less.
- **Check the serving size** -- 2 gummies vs 1 capsule means you're going through the bottle twice as fast.

Whatever form you choose, use our [Supplement Checker](/) to make sure your stack doesn't have any interactions.

title: 11 Things That Deplete Your Magnesium Levels
metaDescription: Stress, caffeine, alcohol, and many medications drain magnesium. Here's what depletes magnesium and how much you need to compensate.
intro: Up to 50% of Americans don't get enough magnesium from diet alone. But even if you eat well, lifestyle factors and medications could be draining your stores. Here are the biggest magnesium killers.

sections.heading: Lifestyle Factors
sections.body:
**1. Stress.** This is the big one. Cortisol causes your kidneys to excrete more magnesium through urine. Chronic stress creates a vicious cycle -- low magnesium makes you more susceptible to stress, which depletes magnesium further.

**2. Caffeine.** Coffee increases urinary magnesium excretion by about 5mg per cup. If you drink 3-4 cups daily, that's 15-20mg lost -- not catastrophic, but it adds up over time.

**3. Alcohol.** Alcohol is one of the most potent magnesium depleters. It increases urinary excretion by up to 260% and also impairs intestinal absorption. Heavy drinkers are almost universally magnesium deficient.

**4. Intense exercise.** You lose magnesium through sweat (about 1-5mg per liter). Heavy training also increases cellular magnesium demand. Athletes need 10-20% more magnesium than sedentary people.

**5. Poor sleep.** Sleep deprivation increases cortisol (see #1). It also reduces the time your body spends in deep sleep, which is when magnesium is best utilized for muscle recovery.

sections.heading: Dietary Factors
sections.body:
**6. Processed food diet.** Food processing strips magnesium. White bread has 80% less magnesium than whole wheat. The modern Western diet is inherently magnesium-poor.

**7. High sugar intake.** Your kidneys excrete more magnesium when processing large amounts of glucose. For every 1% increase in blood sugar, magnesium excretion increases measurably.

**8. High calcium intake.** Calcium and magnesium compete for absorption. If you take high-dose calcium supplements without magnesium, you may be worsening a magnesium deficit.

**9. Soft water.** Hard water is a significant source of dietary magnesium. If your water supply is soft (or you drink filtered/bottled water), you're missing this source.

sections.heading: Medications
sections.body:
**10. Proton pump inhibitors (PPIs).** Omeprazole, esomeprazole, and other acid reflux medications reduce magnesium absorption in the gut. Long-term PPI use is strongly associated with magnesium deficiency. The FDA has issued warnings about this.

**11. Diuretics.** Blood pressure medications like furosemide and hydrochlorothiazide increase urinary magnesium loss. If you take diuretics, your doctor should be monitoring your magnesium levels.

**Other medications that deplete magnesium:**
- Antibiotics (fluoroquinolones, aminoglycosides)
- Oral contraceptives
- Corticosteroids (prednisone)
- Some diabetes medications (insulin can shift magnesium into cells)

sections.heading: Signs You're Depleted
sections.body:
Magnesium deficiency is hard to detect because only 1% of your magnesium is in your blood (the standard test). Symptoms include:

- Muscle cramps and twitches (especially eyelid twitching)
- Poor sleep or insomnia
- Anxiety or racing thoughts
- Heart palpitations
- Fatigue despite adequate sleep
- Headaches
- Restless legs

If you experience multiple symptoms and have 2+ depletion factors from this list, supplementing 300-400mg magnesium glycinate daily is a reasonable approach.

See our guide on [magnesium forms](/blog/magnesium-glycinate-vs-citrate-vs-threonate) to choose the right type, and check interactions with our [Supplement Checker](/).

title: Can Supplements Cause Acne? Which Ones to Watch
metaDescription: Some supplements like B12, biotin, and whey protein can trigger or worsen acne. Here's which ones to watch and what to do about it.
intro: You started a new supplement and your skin broke out. Coincidence? Maybe not. Several popular supplements are linked to acne, and most people have no idea.

sections.heading: Supplements That Can Cause Acne
sections.body:
**Biotin (B7)** -- This is the #1 offender. Biotin competes with vitamin B5 (pantothenic acid) for absorption. B5 is involved in lipid metabolism in the skin. When biotin intake is high and B5 is depleted, sebum production increases and breakouts follow. Doses above 2,500mcg are most problematic.

**Vitamin B12** -- High-dose B12 supplementation can alter the skin microbiome, specifically increasing production of porphyrins by P. acnes bacteria, which triggers inflammation. A 2015 study in Science Translational Medicine demonstrated this mechanism.

**Whey Protein** -- Whey increases insulin and IGF-1 levels, both of which stimulate sebum production and skin cell turnover. Multiple studies link whey protein to acne, especially in young men. Casein is somewhat less problematic.

**DHEA** -- A hormone precursor that converts to androgens. Higher androgen levels directly increase sebum production. Even low doses can cause breakouts in susceptible individuals.

**Iodine (high doses)** -- Excess iodine can trigger acneiform eruptions. Kelp supplements and high-dose iodine are the usual culprits.

sections.heading: Supplements That Help Acne
sections.body:
On the flip side, some supplements actively improve skin:

- **Zinc (30mg)** -- Anti-inflammatory and reduces sebum production. One of the most studied supplements for acne. Zinc picolinate or gluconate are best.
- **Omega-3 (2-3g)** -- Reduces inflammatory markers that contribute to acne. Takes 2-3 months to see effects.
- **Vitamin D** -- Deficiency is associated with increased acne severity. Correcting deficiency can improve skin.
- **Probiotics** -- Gut health directly affects skin (the gut-skin axis). Specific strains like Lactobacillus rhamnosus have shown benefits for acne.
- **Vitamin A** -- Retinol supports skin cell turnover. Don't mega-dose (toxic above 10,000 IU), but adequate intake helps.

sections.heading: What to Do If Supplements Cause Breakouts
sections.body:
**1. Isolate the culprit.** If you started multiple supplements recently, stop them all and reintroduce one at a time, waiting 2 weeks between each.

**2. For biotin-related acne:** Either reduce your dose to under 1,000mcg or add a B5 supplement (500mg pantothenic acid) to offset the competition.

**3. For whey-related acne:** Switch to a plant-based protein (pea, rice) or egg white protein. These don't spike insulin the same way.

**4. For B12-related acne:** Reduce to the RDA (2.4mcg) rather than the mega-doses found in many supplements (1,000-5,000mcg).

**5. Give it time.** Some supplement-induced acne resolves in 2-4 weeks after stopping the offending supplement. If it persists, consult a dermatologist.

Check your supplement stack for other interactions using our [Supplement Checker](/).

title: Ashwagandha vs Rhodiola: Which Adaptogen Is Better?
metaDescription: Ashwagandha and rhodiola are both adaptogens but work differently. Compare their effects on stress, energy, sleep, and performance to pick the right one.
intro: Ashwagandha and rhodiola are the two most popular adaptogens, but they're not interchangeable. One is calming, the other is stimulating. Picking the wrong one can work against your goals.

sections.heading: The Key Difference
sections.body:
**Ashwagandha = calming adaptogen.** It reduces cortisol, promotes relaxation, supports sleep, and works best for chronic stress and anxiety. Think of it as hitting the brakes on your stress response.

**Rhodiola = stimulating adaptogen.** It combats mental fatigue, improves focus under stress, and enhances physical performance. Think of it as giving you more fuel to handle stress without burning out.

This fundamental difference determines which one you should take.

sections.heading: Head-to-Head Comparison
sections.body:
| Factor | Ashwagandha | Rhodiola |
|---|---|---|
| Stress relief | Strong (reduces cortisol 25-30%) | Moderate (improves stress tolerance) |
| Energy | No (mildly sedating) | Yes (anti-fatigue) |
| Sleep | Improves sleep quality | Can disrupt sleep if taken late |
| Anxiety | Strong anxiolytic effect | Mild |
| Focus | Indirect (less anxiety = better focus) | Direct (improves mental performance) |
| Testosterone | Increases (14-17% in studies) | No significant effect |
| Onset | 2-4 weeks for full effect | Days to 1 week |
| Best time | Evening with dinner | Morning on empty stomach |

sections.heading: When to Choose Ashwagandha
sections.body:
Pick ashwagandha if:

- Your main issue is **anxiety or chronic stress**
- You have **sleep problems** related to a racing mind
- You want to support **testosterone levels**
- You're **overtraining** and need recovery support
- You want a **calming** supplement you take daily

**Best form:** KSM-66 extract, 300-600mg daily
**When:** Evening with dinner
**Note:** Consider [cycling](/blog/do-i-need-to-cycle-ashwagandha) (8 weeks on, 2-4 weeks off)

sections.heading: When to Choose Rhodiola
sections.body:
Pick rhodiola if:

- Your main issue is **mental fatigue or burnout**
- You need better **focus and productivity** during stressful periods
- You have a **high-pressure deadline** coming up
- You want **physical endurance** support
- You already sleep fine and don't need more sedation

**Best form:** Standardized to 3% rosavins and 1% salidroside, 200-400mg daily
**When:** Morning on an empty stomach (it's mildly stimulating)
**Note:** Works fast (within days) and is best used situationally during high-stress periods

sections.heading: Can You Take Both?
sections.body:
Yes. They work through different mechanisms and complement each other well:

- **Rhodiola in the morning** for focus and energy
- **Ashwagandha in the evening** for wind-down and sleep

This gives you stress resilience around the clock without the sedation of ashwagandha during work hours or the stimulation of rhodiola at bedtime.

Check how they interact with the rest of your stack using the [Supplement Checker](/).

title: Why Does Iron Make You Constipated (And How to Fix It)
metaDescription: Iron supplements cause constipation in up to 50% of users. Here's why it happens and 5 evidence-based ways to fix it without reducing your dose.
intro: You need iron for your anemia, but now you can't go to the bathroom. Iron-induced constipation affects up to 50% of people who supplement. Here's why it happens and how to fix it.

sections.heading: Why Iron Causes Constipation
sections.body:
Iron is poorly absorbed -- your body only takes in 10-20% of what you ingest. The remaining 80-90% stays in your gut, where it:

**1. Alters gut bacteria.** Unabsorbed iron feeds harmful bacteria in your colon while suppressing beneficial Lactobacillus and Bifidobacterium species. This dysbiosis slows gut motility.

**2. Has a direct astringent effect.** Iron ions tighten intestinal tissues and reduce water secretion into the bowel, making stool harder and slower to pass.

**3. Slows peristalsis.** Iron (especially ferrous sulfate) reduces the rhythmic contractions that move food through your intestines.

The higher the dose and the less bioavailable the form, the worse the constipation.

sections.heading: 5 Ways to Fix It
sections.body:
**1. Switch forms.** Ferrous sulfate (the most common and cheapest) causes the most GI issues. Better-tolerated alternatives:
